clusters.claim: Leaving track structure and entry age unchanged while only redesigning admissions protects current teacher job security and institutional continuity.
A redesigned, less-coachable admission assessment would measure genuine ability rather than purchased test-preparation advantage, serving fairness and meritocracy.

SES-weighted quotas can only improve equity of intake if a valid, administratively workable SES indicator exists and if low-SES/rural/Roma families apply at rates comparable to high-SES families, both currently unverified in Hungary.

Mechanical SES quotas divorce admission from individual merit and risk selecting only the least-disadvantaged among applicants, leaving the most excluded families (no advocacy, no information) still shut out.

The new compliance-audit/monitoring unit and multi-year assessment redesign add administrative, invigilation, and monitoring workload to schools and teachers that is not currently costed or resourced, against a backdrop of documented teacher shortage.

The assessment and quota design process is top-down, naming only ministry-level bodies with no described role for teachers or teacher professional bodies, so it does not expand teacher professional autonomy.

Imposing uniform admissions or quota rules on church and private maintainers by state fiat, without negotiated bilateral agreement, breaches their accord-protected institutional and admissions autonomy.

Families plan years ahead around the current exam format, so an untested redesign of unknown coachability risks making pilot-cohort children guinea pigs and creating admissions uncertainty for those approaching selection age.

Reforming admissions/assessment while leaving track structure untouched matches the teacher union's and the current government's own documented commitments to review admissions and reduce segregation without structural change.
S1 treats the symptom of unfair gatekeeping rather than the underlying mechanism of early selection itself, risking legitimizing early selection as adequately fair without removing it.
Multi-year psychometric validation and phased rollout create admissions unpredictability that undermines multi-year employer and training-provider recruitment planning built around stable criteria.
The felmenő rendszer (grandfathering) clause protects currently-enrolled pupils' places and existing teaching arrangements during the transition period.

The scenario's own uncertainty analysis rates as low-confidence or contested whether general schools can absorb and adequately challenge high-achieving pupils given current teacher shortages, so the mechanism the reform depends on is empirically unproven in Hungary.

Ramping advanced-programme funding without a parallel teacher-recruitment and training pipeline risks asking already shortage-stretched general-school staff to deliver more demanding differentiated instruction without adequate preparation or staffing.
The plan provides no guarantee about post levels, redeployment, job security, or pay for teaching staff in the shrinking selective track once caps tighten.

A gradual, scheduled shrinkage of selective-entry places is effectively a phased abolition of those places and positions, contradicting the teacher union's documented commitment to retain the 6-/8-year tracks.

Shrinking selective places year by year creates a moving target for families with younger children, who cannot know whether a selective place or a genuine nearby advanced-programme alternative will exist by the time it is needed.

The scenario's own political-risk analysis and the Polish reversal precedent show that a phased trajectory without legally entrenched cross-party backing is vulnerable to reversal, undermining confidence to plan around it.

If middle-class families exit to unregulated non-state schools as selective entry shrinks, sorting may simply migrate rather than reduce, concentrating disadvantage among families unable to afford or access non-state alternatives.
A broader base of well-prepared general-school graduates would expand the pool of digitally competent workers for the wider economy, provided the general-school absorption mechanism actually works.

Binding church and private maintainers to an equivalent cap trajectory only through informal negotiation, rather than legislation, either leaves their capacity untouched or asks them to accept a comparable shrinking-capacity trajectory affecting funding and mission without the legislative process applied to state schools.
A statutory, entrenched, credible trajectory toward zero selective-entry places matches the documented structural end-state of a unified school with no early selective entry.

Systematically reducing access to a selective track that families demonstrably value, based on an unconfirmed assumption that general schools can replicate selective-track quality, imposes an immediate, certain choice-loss in exchange for a speculative, delayed benefit.

The government's documented base commits only to admissions-process review and consultation, not to any structural change to track sizes, so adopting a stance on a statutory cap trajectory would assert a position not actually documented.

Removing the formal early-selection mechanism itself, not merely its symptoms, matches the documented structural model of a unified primary school with no selective entry before 14 and is the necessary precondition for efficiently using the whole national talent pool.

The scenario's own cost analysis concedes that nationwide differentiated-instruction teacher retraining is a high, unestimated cost with no specified funding mechanism, timeline certainty, or compulsory/voluntary/salary-linked terms, leaving affected teachers without job-security clarity through a 6-8 year transition.

The reform proceeds against a backdrop of documented Hungarian teacher shortage that the scenario does not itself address, casting doubt on whether the retraining and staffing capacity needed for differentiated instruction can be delivered.

The scenario's own risk analysis names as its largest identified risk that removing the formal selection point may relocate sorting to school choice and non-state maintainers, concentrating the least-mobile, least-informed, poorest families in the weakest comprehensive schools.

The Finnish comparison is a weak analogy because Finland bundled tracking removal with universal teacher retraining, national curriculum standardization, and no private alternative, conditions Hungary's scenario does not replicate, so the underlying peer-effects mechanism remains unconfirmed for Hungary.

The scenario has no described fallback if the cross-party political pact needed to entrench the reform fails, and the Polish reversal precedent shows such pacts are fragile in practice, exposing children, teachers, and institutions mid-transition to a reform that could be partially undone.

A multi-year pilot-then-national-scale-up transition means some children will start school under one structure and finish under another, disrupting routines, friendships, and commuting arrangements, with network reconfiguration directly affecting an unresolved commuting-distance and safety constraint.

Abolishing the 6-/8-year entry point eliminates, for church-maintained gimnáziums, the institutional form itself rather than merely an admissions channel, a right protected by church-state accords, with no described opt-out or negotiated exemption.

The scenario's own political-risk section names church and private maintainer resistance as a driver of the highest-intensity opposition among all four scenarios, itself evidence of the scale of institutional and parental interests being overridden.
PDSZ's documented programme explicitly commits to retaining the 6-/8-year tracks, so S3's abolition of those tracks directly contradicts that documented position.

Nordic-style comprehensive systems are cross-nationally associated with strong average skill and digital-literacy outcomes valuable to a digitalising economy, so there is no principled sectoral objection to later tracking as such.

A comprehensive reform can only deliver its equity promise if teachers are genuinely equipped, resourced, and consulted rather than merely mandated to teach mixed-ability classrooms, without which it risks producing the appearance of reform without real instructional capacity.

The documented model's assumption that comprehensive schools can absorb and challenge high-achievers depends on adequate investment and retraining actually being delivered; persistent implementation failure even under adequate funding would require revisiting implementation assumptions, though not the underlying no-early-selection position.

Abolishing 6-/8-year entry points and moving selection to age 14 is the most structural of the four options, and nothing in the documented government base addresses changing the age of selection or track existence, so asserting any stance here would present extrapolation as government position.

S4 preserves the existing selective-track structure, school identities, and admissions arrangements entirely, producing zero new disruption or transition cost for currently enrolled families, teachers, and institutions, making it the most predictable of the four scenarios.

Of the four scenarios, S4 engages teacher and school material interests most directly through pay premia, recruitment incentives, and clustering support for disadvantaged schools, while avoiding the retraining and redeployment disruption of S2 and S3.

The scenario's own political-risk section states at high confidence that compensation and premia funding is the first line item cut in fiscal consolidation, and that money alone cannot manufacture qualified teachers in a shortage market, making the durability of the package's benefits doubtful.

A teacher or family who commits to a disadvantaged post or expectation of improvement on the strength of a compensation package that is later cut is left worse off than if the promise had never been made.

S4 treats the symptom of under-resourced general schools rather than the underlying early age-10/12 selection point, and no comparator country has shown compensation fully offsetting selection effects this early, making the Portuguese transfer speculative for Hungary.

By making the existing tracked structure more fiscally and politically tolerable through compensation, S4 risks entrenching early selection as permanent, foreclosing political space for structural reform.
S4 is the only scenario that imposes no admissions constraints, caps, or structural conversion on non-state maintainers, leaving their institutional autonomy and continuity fully intact.

The scenario's proposed standardized national curriculum expectations across all tracks could be read to override denominational schools' accord-protected religious-education content, or to pull toward more centralized prescription of instructional methods in tension with teacher professional autonomy.

S4 omits the admissions-reform plank that the teacher union's documented programme also requires alongside retaining tracks and reducing segregation, making it only a partial match to that documented position on its own.

S4 matches the government's revealed preference, shown by its consistent avoidance of structural commitment while naming the SES-skew problem, for non-structural remedies that leave the existing track structure intact.

S4 could be acceptable as a parallel, transitional measure bundled with binding structural reform, but not as a permanent standalone substitute, since as a substitute it legitimises the architecture of exclusion itself.

The Portuguese TEIP model demonstrates that targeted funding, curriculum standards, and school clustering can produce measurable PISA gains, supporting targeted investment as a legitimate, less disruptive public role compared to structural dismantling.
